**Timeline Analysis:**

| Week | W1 (crane) | W2 (crane) | W3 (crane) | W4 (no crane) | W5 (crane) | Cranes in use |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------------|-----------|---------------|
| 1-6  | Running   | Running   | Running   | Running      | —         | 3/3           |
| 7-8  | Running   | Running   | Running   | Done (wk 6)  | —         | 3/3           |
| 8    | **Finishes** | Running | Running  | Done         | —         | 2/3 (crane freed) |
| 9-12 | Done      | Running   | Running   | Done         | **Starts wk 8** | 3/3     |
| 13-14| Done      | Done (wk 12) | Done (wk 10) | Done    | Running   | 1/3           |

**Key facts:**
- W5 starts at week 8 (picks up W1's freed crane)
- W5 duration = 6 weeks → finishes at **week 14**
- The original final phase was scheduled to start at week 12

**No, W5 cannot complete within the original timeline.** W5 finishes at week 14, which is 2 weeks past the planned start of the final phase. The new critical path becomes:

- **W5 completes:** week 14
- **Final phase:** weeks 14–18
- **New total:** 18 weeks (up from the original 16)

The project manager's original 16-week estimate assumed no W5. Adding W5 extends the project by **2 weeks** to **18 weeks total**.
